I was cleaning out my closet and decided to try on all my jeans and see what fit. As I went along, I realized a lot weren’t fitting me anymore. I am not training full time right now and am not in Olympic Sevens shape. In my head, I want to be that size and be as I fit as I was but it’s not realistic for me. I’m trying to give myself some grace. Remember that my body is going to constantly change. Right now, I feel a little bigger but I’m being kind to myself. Giving her (my body) time to just be. I know that I was get back in Olympic shape and I also know that I will get out of it too. That’s life. That’s the journey our bodies will go on. So for now my belly stays and the jeans will go.

I’ve always been a fan of the quote “life begins at the end of your comfort zone”. It’s been so fun to continuously find where the end is and cross it by miles. Dancing live on TV was one thing and then agreeing to dance some more in front of sold out crowds was another. It was tough. Doing something that feels so foreign and uncomfortable in your body in front of thousands of people will test you in many ways. At one of the shows, I remember thinking to myself, “why the heck am I doing this?” I felt so nervous and anxious. I can barely count the music and the steps never seem to fall into place. But I did it. Life would be easy if I said no to things that scared me but that’s not really how the best memories are made. I joke about retiring from dance but it taught me a lot. I feel like I’m closing my chapter on Dancing with the Stars. I will forever be grateful for this amazing show and everything it gave me.

95% of the comments I get are amazing. They make me feel good. They praise my physique and personality. They are uplifting.  But there are still the 5% that seek to be mean and bring me down. I don’t like to give them attention but I also know that if I am getting these comments so are other beautiful girls and women.  I share this because it doesn’t matter how many pretty pictures I post or how dominant I am on the rugby pitch, people will always find something negative to say. Those people are sad, insecure, and looking for any way to somehow make themselves feel good. Do not listen or put stock in what these people say.  It’s a hard truth that these comments will never stop but what should stop is your belief in them. Choose to listen to your friends and family around you who actually know you. Who see the real you. And just maybe start to believe that the 95% is real because I’m no mathematician but those odds seem good.
